summary: Jeff Atwood explores the key factors that determine whether software projects succeed or fail. He starts with Steve McConnell's hierarchy—project size, type of software, and personnel—then adds Bill de hÓra's 'three pillars' of version control, work item tracking, and build systems as equally critical infrastructure. The post also touches on framework choice, noting that frameworks designed for the web rather than around it dramatically impact productivity. Ultimately, Atwood concludes that project success isn't about doing one thing right, but about the daunting task of not doing anything wrong across multiple dimensions.
key_points: Project size is the single most significant determinant of software project success—large projects tend to fail
The 'three pillars' of version control, work item tracking, and build systems are first-order effects that impact everything else on a project
Framework and tool choices matter enormously—frameworks designed for the web outperform those that treat the web as something to work around
IDEs and even methodology choice may matter less than getting the fundamental infrastructure right
Software project success requires not doing anything wrong across multiple factors, not just excelling at one thing
money_quotes: Most software projects fail. But that doesn't mean yours has to.
Size is easily the most significant determinant of effort, cost, and schedule.
The version control system is a first order effect on software, along with two others – the build system and the bugtracker.
Things like IDEs, by comparison, don't matter at all. Even choice of methodology might matter less.
I suspect designing for the Web instead of around it is at least as important as language choice.
Maybe the real lesson here is that software project success isn't about doing any one particular thing right; it's the much more daunting task of not doing anything wrong.
key_insight: Software project success requires systematically avoiding failure across multiple dimensions—size, team, infrastructure, and framework choice—rather than excelling at any single factor.
